title: Easter Sunday
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## Easter Sunday, April 4, 2021 | Preacher: Richard Lawson

---

LITURGICAL CONTEXT

Easter Sunday 2021 falls on April 4, marking the principal feast of the Christian year celebrating Christ's resurrection. The RCL readings are Isaiah 65:17-25, Psalm 118:1-2, 14-24, 1 Corinthians 15:1-11, and John 20:1-18 (or Luke 24:1-12 as alternative Gospel). This is the culmination of the Lenten season and the beginning of the 50-day Easter season, making it the most significant liturgical moment in the Episcopal calendar.

---

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was navigating the continued fallout from the 2018 General Convention's decision on human sexuality and marriage equality, with implementation debates ongoing in various dioceses. The Diocese of Colorado was in relative stability but continued working through pandemic-related liturgical adaptations that began in 2020. No major Episcopal denominational controversies or bishop elections directly centered on April 2021, though the broader church remained engaged with racial justice conversations initiated post-2020.

---

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was emerging from winter weather patterns typical for early April. The primary local focus was on Colorado's ongoing COVID-19 vaccination rollout—the state had recently expanded eligibility and was vaccinating roughly 40,000-50,000 people daily by early April 2021. The Capitol Hill neighborhood, where Saint John's Cathedral is located, was beginning to show signs of renewed street activity as vaccinations increased and pandemic restrictions loosened slightly, though outdoor dining and limited indoor capacity remained the norm.

---

NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The United States was in mid-vaccination campaign with roughly 30% of Americans fully vaccinated by early April 2021. President Biden had announced a May 1st deadline for U.S. troop withdrawal from Afghanistan after 20 years, generating significant political debate. The Derek Chauvin trial had concluded on April 20, 2021 (so this is one week before that verdict), but the trial itself was ongoing and a dominant national news story. Former President Trump's second impeachment trial had recently concluded in February, and the Republican Party was navigating post-Trump identity questions.

---

INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The global COVID-19 pandemic remained the dominant international story, with India beginning to experience a devastating second wave of infections in late March/early April 2021. The European Union was debating vaccine rollout strategies and vaccine passports amid concerns about supply delays. Myanmar's military coup (February 2021) continued to generate international attention with ongoing civil disobedience and protest movements.

---

CULTURAL MOMENT

The United States was at a pivot point psychologically—one year into the pandemic with vaccines offering genuine hope, yet continued uncertainty about timeline and normalcy. There was palpable tension between desire to return to pre-pandemic life and ongoing precaution. The cultural mood mixed cautious optimism with lingering grief and exhaustion. The NCAA March Madness tournament had just concluded (Baylor won the men's championship on April 5), and pop culture was dominated by streaming shows (The Falcon and the Winter Soldier, for Marvel fans) and the continued cultural conversation around race sparked by George Floyd's death anniversary approaching in May.
